headlineSummary: HN sentiment was mixed overall: OpenAI and Anthropic were close to neutral with a close tie between them, while Microsoft Copilot had limited evidence leaning negative [E3][E8][E13].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot drew a mildly negative reaction, but the position rests on limited evidence from one comment about frustrating daily prompts to use it in Office [E13].

Anthropic landed in mixed-neutral territory with high support: there was both strong praise for Claude's usefulness and quality and strong criticism about poor quality and an outage, plus some mixed or mildly positive notes [E8][E9][E10][E12].

OpenAI was also mixed-neutral, with several positive comments about Codex's release, free access, and usefulness offset by critiques of mobile results and ChatGPT's capabilities, and it was in a close tie with Anthropic [E1][E2][E4][E5][E6][E7].

evidence.excerpt: Codex is now in the ChatGPT mobile app

annotations.rationale: The title is a factual product-release announcement about Codex and ChatGPT, both OpenAI aliases, without evaluative language.

evidence.excerpt: Whats crazier is that Codex is free. I thought I had to pay to even try it out but nope, you can use the desktop app or cli for free, its apparently included in the free plan.

annotations.rationale: The commenter praises Codex being free and included in the free plan, which is favorable sentiment about OpenAI's pricing/access.

evidence.excerpt: Codex has been great in the last 3-4 months I've been using it, almost exclusively to review existing GDScript code, and this was the feature I wanted most, because with gamedev you get the best ideas when you're out and about or in bed :) Claude on the other hand has been jank all around from the UX to the UI to the AI itself that it's baffling how it's more popular here on HN

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Codex "has been great" and calls this the feature they wanted most.
The commenter explicitly criticizes Claude as "jank all around" across UX, UI, and AI quality.

evidence.excerpt: I’ve been using Codex from my phone for the past couple of months (through a tunnel, not this app). I was initially quite excited, but I’ve found the results are less than great compared to being at a keyboard.

annotations.rationale: The commenter reports disappointment with mobile Codex results, saying they are "less than great".

evidence.excerpt: This is extremely what Ive been wanting -- I had previously thought about using one of the hackish apps that try to deliver this experience - or spinning up something for this myself ... - but integrating this directly is definitely the right way to provide the best system and product experience -- and this seems to work out of the box exactly as I would want!

annotations.rationale: In the Codex/ChatGPT story context, the commenter strongly endorses the integration as "definitely the right way" and says it works exactly as desired.

evidence.excerpt: This is awesome. Exactly what openclaw was supposed to be. Im really impressed by how effortlessly it handles the file syncing.

annotations.rationale: Within the Codex mobile-app story context, the commenter gives strong praise, calling it "awesome" and saying they are "really impressed" by the file syncing.

evidence.excerpt: It takes an army of paid data producers solving homework problems to give ChatGPT the ability to do your homework.

annotations.rationale: This is a strongly critical claim that ChatGPT's capability is derivative and dependent on large-scale paid data generation.

evidence.excerpt: On the flipside, I love using Claude (in moderation). It keeps pulling off several very nice things, some of which Mitchell touched on in this post (the last one): - I write scripts and automation from time to time; Claude fleshes them out way better with way more safety features, feature flags, and logging than I'd otherwise have capacity to spend time on - Claude catches missed refactors and preexisting defects, and does a generally solid pass checking for defects as a whole

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says they "love using Claude" and lists multiple concrete quality benefits.

evidence.excerpt: overall quality is bad, and we had our first real production outage that Claude one-shot caused but could not one-shot solve.

annotations.rationale: The commenter attributes a real production outage to Claude and says overall quality is bad, which is strong criticism.

evidence.excerpt: creating recipes with Claude Opus based on flavor profiles and preferences feels magical, right up until the point at which it can't accurately convert between tablespoons and teaspoons.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt combines praise ("feels magical") with a concrete failure on basic facts, making the overall stance mixed.

evidence.excerpt: > I’m stepping back from full-time Zulip leadership to join Anthropic, alongside three senior team members, and we’re donating the company to a newly created, independent, nonprofit Zulip Foundation

annotations.rationale: This is a factual statement about team members joining Anthropic, without clear praise or criticism.

evidence.excerpt: Reads like Anthropic has recognized that in order to better get into enterprise it needs to be better than Slack - meet users where they are most of the time. Having someone experienced with a good reputation and a say on how to steer a product sounds quite beneficial.

annotations.rationale: The commenter frames Anthropic's move as strategically smart and "quite beneficial," which is mild praise.

evidence.excerpt: They send us angry reminders if we don't use copilot in ms office every day :( I just type Hello to it.

annotations.rationale: The commenter expresses annoyance at daily pressure to use Copilot in MS Office, which is a negative reaction tied to the product's rollout/adoption.
